upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a pronounced low-level inversion capping the marine boundary layer: surface temps are 12.4°C at 3m but jump to 17°C at 850mb (~1540m), indicating a strong subsidence inversion near 1500–1550m MSL that will cap thermal development well below the summit of the higher inland hills. The surface-to-850mb lapse rate of only -3.04°C/km is extremely stable (far below dry adiabatic 9.8°C/km), meaning thermals will be weak and capped — expect modest boundary layer heights of 300–500m AGL this afternoon consistent with model data. Upper winds veer from 180° at surface to 295° at 850mb and 255° at 700mb, with 43 kts at 500mb, suggesting strong upper-level flow but light and variable surface winds transitioning to a sea breeze NW regime by midday.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KSFO showing a wind shift (WSHFT 2029Z) with 28–30 kts gusts out of 280–300° — confirms active sea breeze onset on the coast this afternoon. KHAF reporting 330–340° at 10–15G17 kts, clear skies, 10SM — no marine layer at Half Moon Bay, coast is clear and dry. KRHV reporting 310° at 9 kts, SKC, 27°C — inland valley warm and dry, consistent with thermal afternoon. KAPC 250° at 10 kts, CLR, 26°C — Napa also warm, confirming inland heating. KWVI light southerly/westerly, CLR — no anomalies.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NW-facing launch, thermal/ridge hybrid site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750 ft launch relies on afternoon valley thermals combining with a sea breeze NW flow to produce soarable conditions. Ideal winds are 8–15 kts from 300–340° NW; too light and thermals collapse quickly, too strong and the upper ridge becomes turbulent.
sites.todaySetup: Today's story at the 1750 ft launch is a pleasant but modest thermal day constrained by the strong subsidence inversion sitting near 1500m MSL. The inversion hard-caps thermal height — expect climbs to 400–500m AGL at best, roughly 1000–1200 ft above launch. Winds are the saving grace: light and variable this morning (3–5 kts, N/NW) transitioning to a consistent NW sea breeze of 6–8 kts by afternoon, well within flyable range. GFS and HRRR are in full agreement on today's data. Zero CAPE, zero precip, clear skies, and warm temps at KRHV (27°C) confirm solid heating for weak-to-moderate thermals. The window is mid-to-late afternoon before the BL collapses back after 18:00.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light 3–5 kts N/NE, boundary layer only 65–305m. Too light and thermally underdeveloped for the top launch — wait for heating.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds building NW 5–6 kts, BL rising to 355–455m AGL. Thermals firing but weak; soarable XC low expectations, good for scratchy top-to-bottom flights.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW sea breeze steady at 6–8 kts through 17:00, BL peaking ~455m AGL at 14:00 then slowly dropping. Best thermal window; conditions mellow out after 18:00 as BL collapses.

verdict.hazards: Inversion caps thermals ~400–500m AGL; light morning winds may feel lifeless pre-noon; BL collapses after 7 PM

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-hill launch, NW-facing, lower training hill
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is the intermediate hill at Ed Levin, suitable for sled rides and low soaring in light NW flow. It needs 5–10 kts NW to stay up and benefits from afternoon valley thermal cycles that push air up the face.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch sees the same NW sea breeze cycle as the top launch but from a lower perspective. The key issue here is the morning transition: winds are nearly calm or variable at the 600 ft level through 10 AM (0.4 kts from 153° at 10:00 is almost dead calm with a slight southerly component — not flyable), but by noon the NW sea breeze fills in at 4–5 kts, building to 7–8 kts by 15:00–16:00. Clear skies, zero cloud, and warm valley temps support light afternoon thermals. The BL grows to 460–470m, comfortably above this launch elevation. With 7–8 kts NW in the afternoon, this becomes a viable soaring window even from the lower hill.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind nearly calm and variable (0.4–1.7 kts, 153–306°) — unflyable, no consistent flow on the face.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW flow arrives 4–5 kts by noon, building steadily. Light soaring possible, thermal cycle developing.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window with 7–8 kts NW at 15:00–17:00, BL at 435–460m. Soarable sled rides and thermal top-ups; winds ease after 18:00.

verdict.hazards: Dead calm and variable before noon — do not launch early; late PM BL collapse; rotor possible below launch if sea breeze overshoots

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, NW/W-facing, sled ride site
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft training hill is primarily a sled-ride site for student and novice pilots, occasionally soarable in light NW thermic flow. Needs smooth 5–8 kts NW to stay aloft; very sensitive to gusty or cross conditions.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft launch has the most variable and potentially challenging wind pattern today. This morning sees near-calm to southerly winds (1 kt from 191° at 8 AM, 0.7 kts at 9 AM), which are unflyable. The interesting dynamic here is that the 300 ft level is in the valley floor transition zone — winds can be cross or variable as the sea breeze front advances through the valley. By noon, WNW flow is established at 5–7 kts and builds to 7–9 kts NW by 13:00–16:00, which is actually the ideal soaring range for this hill. However, at 8–9 kts the conditions may feel punchy for true beginners. Afternoon BL of 455–515m AGL provides a decent thermal ceiling well above this launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Near calm and southerly/variable — completely unflyable at this level. Wait for sea breeze to fill in.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW flow building 5–7 kts by noon; flyable for P2+ once direction stabilizes NW. Watch for gusty transitions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Steady NW 7–9 kts, 13:00–17:00 is the prime window. Can be slightly punchy for novices; experienced P2s fine. BL peaks at 515m.

verdict.hazards: Variable/southerly winds before noon; afternoon flow 8–9 kts may be punchy for beginners; confirm direction is stable NW before launching

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-lift site powered by the Pacific onshore sea breeze. It flies best with 12–20 kts from 270–320° W/NW; too light (under 8 kts) and there's no ridge lift, too strong (over 25 kts) and it becomes dangerous with rotor behind the ridge.
sites.todaySetup: Today Mussel Rock is the most interesting site in the brief — the conditions are real but on the light side of ridge soaring. KHAF METARs show 330–340° at 10–15G17 kts, confirming the NNW sea breeze is already active at the coast right now. Buoy data supports this: SF Bar buoy at 11.7 kts from 320°, Half Moon Bay buoy at 7.8 kts from 320° — consistent NW flow with no marine layer (KHAF 10SM CLR). Cold SST of 12.3–13.3°C and wave period of 11s suggest a healthy sea breeze setup without a deep foggy marine layer. Models show 5–7 kts at 10m building through the afternoon to 6–7 kts. The caution is that surface winds at the model grid are somewhat light (5–7 kts) while the 80m level shows 8–9 kts — the bluff face should accelerate and concentrate flow, likely pushing effective ridge-face winds to the 10–15 kt range. GFS and HRRR agree perfectly for today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 2–4 kts at model level, but KHAF METAR already shows 10–15 kts NNW at 2155Z — the ridge face likely already has some lift. Light to marginal soaring possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Model winds building to 5–6 kts at surface, 6–9 kts at 80m. With coastal acceleration on the bluff face, likely 10–14 kts effective — good ridge soaring window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window with 6–7 kts model surface (9 kts at 80m), direction locked in at 295–305° W/NW. KSFO wind shift to 28–30 kts confirms healthy afternoon sea breeze. Smooth soaring expected.

verdict.hazards: Light rotor risk below bluff in northerly component; 80m winds notably stronger than surface — watch for increasing gradient; no marine fog today

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/peak, NW-facing thermal launch, 2517 ft summit
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak relies on a combination of valley thermals from the warm East Bay and an afternoon NW sea breeze that wraps around the peak. Ideal conditions are 8–15 kts NW with active thermals; the peak's elevation means pilots need to commit to the climb or have a solid LZ plan.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak today mirrors Ed Levin's setup — same sea breeze NW regime, same capped thermals — but with a notable quirk in the 120m wind data showing increasing upper-level flow. The surface and 80m winds are nearly identical to Ed Levin (light N/NW morning, 5–8 kts NW afternoon), but the Wind120m layer shows 10–13 kts by 13:00–17:00, indicating the gradient tightens aloft. This is worth watching for mechanical turbulence near the peak's exposed summit. The sounding inversion at ~1550m MSL caps thermals below the summit, meaning thermal tops will be below or near launch elevation — expect weak scratchy thermals rather than booming climbs. Zero CAPE, clear skies, and warm KRHV temps (27°C) confirm flyable but modest thermal conditions. The BL peaks at 455–515m AGL in the afternoon.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light 3–4 kts N, BL only 65–305m AGL. Too early and thermally underdeveloped — peak feels dead before 11 AM.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW 5–6 kts, BL to 355–455m. Thermals starting; flyable with modest climbs. 120m winds already 6–10 kts — check for turbulence near summit.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW 6–8 kts at 80m, 10–13 kts at 120m through 17:00. Best soaring window but the increasing upper gradient may produce mechanical chop near the summit; smooth lower-altitude flying likely fine.

verdict.hazards: Inversion caps thermals near or below launch altitude; 120m winds 10–13 kts may produce summit mechanical turbulence; commit to launch only when thermals confirmed active

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Wednesday March 25 looks windier and cloudier. GFS/HRRR both show 100% cloud cover most of the day, NW winds building to 9–10 kts by afternoon with 80m winds at 10–12 kts. No precip but overcast skies kill thermal development (CAPE=0). Marginal at best — heavy cloud suppresses heating, winds borderline strong.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to the top launch: 100% overcast most of the day, winds 9–11 kts NW by afternoon, 80m at 11–14 kts. Too much cloud for thermals, wind on the stronger side. Marginal/No Fly.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Overcast all day, 10–11 kts NW afternoon, gusty at 80m (11–13 kts). No thermal support under solid cloud. Not recommended.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow is a strong NW wind day at Mussel Rock — potentially excellent ridge soaring but with caveats. GFS/HRRR show 15–18 kts at surface building to 21–26 kts at 80m by afternoon. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 17–20 kts by 16–18Z with 100% cloud. The 80m winds of 24–26 kts are concerning for upper ridge turbulence. Strong/expert day — P3+ or P4 locals only in afternoon. Morning window (7.6 kts at 8Z) may be more manageable.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Overcast 94–100%, NW winds 7–10 kts surface, 8–12 kts at 80m. No thermals under overcast. Marginal for ridge-style soaring only; thermals suppressed.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Thursday March 26 is complicated: morning shows 14 kts from 034° (NE) at 10m but a stunning 27 kts at 80m — likely post-frontal or offshore flow. By midday winds shift to 339° N at 6 kts and the BL opens to 745–810m, highest of the 4-day period. 100% cloud all day. Thermal potential improves with deeper BL but persistent overcast limits heating. Worth watching — could be a soarable deep BL day if clouds thin.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same frontal signature: 15.7 kts NE at 8Z with 26 kts at 80m — not flyable in the morning. Clears to 6 kts N by noon, BL 745–810m. 100% cloud persists. Deep BL but overcast; similar to top launch.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Morning 2.4 kts from 305° but 80m showing 10.7 kts — shear flag. By noon winds normalize to 6 kts N, deep BL. 100% cloud. Not recommended due to cloud cover and morning shear.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wind direction goes variable/NE/N in the morning (3.6 kts from 112°) — wrong direction for ridge lift. By midday shifts to 024° N at 8 kts, 80m at 12 kts. Afternoon returns to NW/W around 294°. Transitional day with variable directions — difficult to plan. 100% cloud throughout.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Morning 14 kts from 034°, 80m at 27 kts — not flyable. By midday NNE 6 kts, BL rises to 670–805m, deep for the season. 100% cloud suppresses thermal quality. Monitor but cloud cover is the limiter.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Friday March 27 shows near-calm morning (1.5 kts, 40°), transitioning to light NW 4–6 kts by afternoon, BL growing to 490–780m — the deepest of the week. 100% cloud all day dampens thermals, but a tiny CAPE signal (30 J/kg) appears at 18Z suggesting possible instability late in the day. Overall light-wind overcast day; marginal.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Similar to top launch: light winds, 100% cloud, BL 490–780m. Cloud cover continues to be the limiting factor all week.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Light winds, deep BL (490–780m), 100% cloud. Same story as other Ed Levin elevations.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Friday shows light NE/N winds in the morning (3.8–5.2 kts from 060–056°) — wrong direction for ridge soaring. By afternoon shifts back toward NW (317° at 5 kts). Cloud cover 100% all day. Light wind day at the coast; poor ridge soaring prospects.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Very light winds all day (2–4 kts), BL 495–845m — deepest of the period. 100% cloud. Light and lifeless conditions; not worth the hike.

watchlist: Tomorrow (March 25) Mussel Rock: 80m winds building to 21–26 kts with gusts to 20 kts per Tomorrow.io — strong/expert conditions only; monitor for whether surface winds stay manageable in the morning
March 26 morning: 27 kts at 80m over Ed Levin with NE surface winds suggests possible post-frontal gap flow or offshore surge — unusual shear signature worth monitoring before planning any flights
Persistent 100% cloud cover Wednesday through Friday will suppress thermals at all inland sites regardless of wind — do not count on thermal soaring Wed-Fri
SST at 12.3–13.3°C with 11s wave period confirms active NW swell and sea breeze regime — Mussel Rock ridge soaring window may extend later into the week if winds moderate
KRHV showing 27°C and SKC today confirms excellent inland heating for thermal sites this afternoon — best flying day of the 4-day period is today
tldr: Today (Tuesday March 24) is the best flying day of the week — head to Ed Levin top launch or Mussel Rock this afternoon for a clean NW sea breeze window from roughly 1–5 PM. Clear skies, 6–8 kts NW, and warm valley temps make for solid if shallow soaring (thermals capped ~400–500m AGL by a strong inversion). Wednesday through Friday brings increasing overcast and stronger winds that progressively shut down the thermal sites.
